title: Sausage Roll
subtitle: Pork sausagemeat with sage and onion in buttery puff pastry
excerpt: A properly made sausage roll uses good sausagemeat, a little sage and onion for flavour, and a sheet of puff pastry rolled tight and brushed with egg. The result is miles from a frozen party snack.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: sausage-meat rolls entry. Method consistent with British picnic and luncheon tradition documented across Victorian household manuals.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled, uncooked rolls on a tray until solid, then transfer to bags. Bake from frozen at 190°C for 35 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the batch; freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerate unbaked. Brush with egg just before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beaten egg brushed over pastry or dough before baking to give a rich golden colour and a slight sheen. A splash of milk beaten with the egg extends the wash further.

content.text: Sausage rolls are sausagemeat enclosed in puff pastry and baked until golden. The quality lives in the sausagemeat: buy good pork sausages and squeeze the meat out, or use loose sausagemeat from a butcher. Sage and onion sharpens the filling; a beaten-egg wash makes the pastry gleam.

items.prepNote: finely grated or very finely diced

content.text: Mix the sausagemeat with the finely chopped sage, grated onion, mustard if using, and a few grinds of black pepper. Work it together briefly with your hands; it only needs to be combined, not over-mixed.
Heat the oven to 200°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Unroll the puff pastry on a lightly floured surface. Cut it in half lengthways to give two long strips, each about 12 cm wide.

Divide the sausagemeat into two halves. Roll each half into a log shape and lay it along the centre of one pastry strip. The sausagemeat log should be about 4 cm wide. Brush one long edge of the pastry with
and fold the other edge over the sausagemeat to enclose it. Press firmly to seal, then turn seam-side down.

Brush the tops with egg wash. Cut each roll into 6 pieces for large rolls or 12 for cocktail size. Space on the lined tray with the cuts facing upward. Score the top of each roll twice with a sharp knife.
Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until deep golden and cooked through. The sausagemeat should show no pink when cut; if in doubt, check one roll. Rest for 5 minutes on the tray before serving.

items.cause: Fat from the sausagemeat seeped through during baking before the base set.
items.fix: Use a hot oven from the start and bake on the bottom shelf for the first 10 minutes. A preheated tray also helps.

items.symptom: Sausagemeat is underdone in the centre
items.cause: Rolls were too thick, or oven temperature dropped.
items.fix: Keep the sausagemeat log to 4 cm diameter. Check one roll from each batch; the sausagemeat should be fully opaque and no longer pink.

items.cause: The seam was not pressed firmly enough.
items.fix: Press hard when sealing, and ensure the seam is firmly on the bottom when placed on the tray. Egg wash on the join helps it stick.

items.cause: Over-mixed, or the sausagemeat was too lean.
items.fix: Mix just until combined. Use sausagemeat with a decent fat content; lean mince will always be dry.

content.text: The sausage roll has been part of British snack food since at least the mid-19th century, when the Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton includes a version under the name sausage-meat rolls. It is picnic food, party food, and a reliable thing to bring to a gathering when you want something that will disappear quickly. The version sold in bakeries is usually blander than a homemade one. Use proper pork sausagemeat with a good fat percentage and the difference is immediately obvious.
